user_request: Draft a civil suit for recovery of Rs.10,00,000/- where the Defendant issued a cheque dated 15.01.2025 for Rs.10,00,000/- drawn on State Bank of India which was dishonoured on presentation with endorsement 'Insufficient Funds'. A legal notice under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act was sent on 01.02.2025 but the Defendant failed to pay within 15 days. Plead under Section 138 NI Act and also claim civil recovery with interest at 18% per annum. Include cause of action, limitation, valuation, court fee and verification clause. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

facts.summary: The plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 10,00,000/- based on a cheque issued by the defendant on 15.01.2025, drawn on State Bank of India, which was dishonoured due to insufficient funds. A legal notice under Section 138 NI Act was sent on 01.02.2025, and the defendant failed to pay within the stipulated 15 days.

chronology.event: Defendant issued cheque for Rs. 10,00,000/- drawn on State Bank of India
Cheque presented for payment and dishonoured with endorsement 'Insufficient Funds'
Legal notice under Section 138 NI Act sent to Defendant
Defendant failed to pay within 15 days of notice

slots.value: Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act

missing_fields: Plaintiff and Defendant names and addresses
Details of legal notice dispatch (mode, reference number)
assumptions: The cause of action arose within the limitation period for a civil suit (3 years from dishonour/notice expiry).

under this section has to be exercised judicially and not automatically for the asking and that negligence of the 
party or his legal adviser or his clerk can be no good ground for the exercise of this discretion. 80 The Allahabad

sections.instruction: Draft the suit title line based on the nature of the suit, relief sought, and any monetary amount involved. Use standard Indian court pleading format. If this is a property suit use 'SUIT FOR PARTITION AND SEPARATE POSSESSION' style. If recovery suit use 'SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/-' style. Adapt to the actual suit type from the classified doc_type and intake facts.

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff(s) and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, address. Use competency averment (sui juris / competent to sue). If any detail is missing, use {{PLACEHOLDER}}. Adapt party labels to the suit type (e.g., Petitioner/Respondent for writ petitions).

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction based on the facts. For territorial jurisdiction, state the SPECIFIC factual basis: where the defendant resides, where property is situated, where the cause of action arose, or where the transaction occurred. For suits involving immovable property, jurisdiction is based on where the property is situated (Section 16 CPC). For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the suit valuation and that it falls within the court's monetary limits.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Adapt the narrative to the actual nature of the suit: for property suits describe ownership chain and possession history, for recovery suits describe the transaction and default, for family matters describe relationships and events. Reference documents as Annexure labels. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

must_include.description: At least one date or date placeholder

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section citing relevant statutory provisions and legal principles. For EACH provision: (1) State the legal rule it creates, (2) State what conditions trigger it, (3) Show how the facts satisfy those conditions. Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list or those naturally applicable to the suit type. If applicable, include alternative pleas using 'In the alternative and without prejudice...' framing. Include defensive pleading to pre-emptively neutralize likely defense arguments.

must_include.description: At least one statutory provision cited

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose. Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose (the specific event: default, refusal, denial, dispossession, etc.), (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — subsequent acts that sustain the right (further demands, further refusals), (3) CONTINUITY — why the cause of action is continuing. Anchor to the actual triggering event from the facts, not to procedural dates like notice.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act 1963 (from enrichment or use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation anchored to the cause of action accrual event, (4) that the suit is within time.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section. State the suit valuation and basis (claim amount for money suits, property value for property suits, etc.). State court fee as per applicable State Court Fees Act or use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder. For property suits, state valuation per Section 7 of the Court Fees Act (market value of share) if applicable.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section IF applicable to this suit type. For money recovery suits: pre-suit interest, pendente lite interest (Section 34 CPC), and future interest with rates and justification. For property suits: mesne profits or compensation if applicable, or state that interest is not the primary relief. Adapt to the actual nature of the suit. If interest is not relevant to this suit type, state briefly that the Plaintiff claims mesne profits / compensation as applicable, or omit interest entirely and instead address any financial relief that fits.

must_include.description: Some form of financial relief or interest stated

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must be specific and tied to the relief sought. Adapt prayers to the actual suit type: for partition suits seek preliminary decree declaring share + final decree for division + separate possession + commissioner appointment; for recovery suits seek decree for principal amount + interest + costs; for injunction suits seek permanent injunction + damages. Always include a general relief clause 'any other relief the Court deems fit'.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon. Map to annexure labels. Adapt documents to the suit type: for property suits include title deeds, genealogy, revenue records, survey sketches; for recovery suits include agreement, payment proof, notice; for family suits include marriage certificate, birth records. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents for this suit type with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

sections.heading: VERIFICATION
sections.body:
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

filled_sections.heading: BETWEEN
filled_sections.text: Plaintiff: {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}} (hereinafter referred to as the 'Plaintiff'), is sui juris and competent to sue. Defendant: {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}} (hereinafter referred to as the 'Defendant'), is competent to be sued. The Plaintiff files this suit for recovery of Rs. 10,00,000/- arising from a cheque dated 15.01.2025 drawn on State Bank of India by the Defendant, which on presentation was dishonoured with the endorsement 'Insufficient Funds'. On 01.02.2025 the Plaintiff served a legal notice under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act; however, the Defendant failed to make payment within the statutory period of 15 days.

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
1. On {{DATE}}, the Defendant issued to the Plaintiff a cheque for Rs. 10,00,000 drawn on the State Bank of India and handed over the instrument at {{PLACE_NAME}} in discharge of the Defendant's obligation under their financial arrangement. (Annexure A)

2. On a date prior to {{DATE}}, the Plaintiff presented the cheque for payment to the drawee bank, State Bank of India, at {{CHEQUE_PRESENTATION_PLACE}}, whereupon the bank returned the instrument unpaid with the endorsement "Insufficient Funds" on the dishonour memo dated {{DISHONOR_DATE}}. (Annexure B)

3. On {{DATE}}, the Plaintiff served the Defendant with a legal notice under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act at the Defendant's address {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}} demanding payment of Rs. 10,00,000 within 15 days. The notice is evidenced by Annexure C and service by delivery/acknowledgement is evidenced by Annexure D.

4. Despite service, the Defendant did not pay the amount within the statutory period, i.e., no payment was received by {{DATE}}, and the outstanding sum remains unpaid. (Annexure D)

5. On {{FILING_DATE}}, the Plaintiff filed this suit before the District Court at {{PLACE_NAME}} seeking recovery of Rs. 10,00,000 and interest at 18% per annum from the date of default, relying on the facts set out above. The Plaintiff has valued the suit at Rs. 10,00,000 for purposes of court fee and jurisdiction, has paid the prescribed court fee, and has appended a verification clause to the plaint. Annexures E (Verification) and F (Court Fee and Valuation) are attached to support these procedural aspects. (Annexure E, Annexure F)

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text:
LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

This suit is founded on the statutory remedy available under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, read with the ordinary civil law governing money recovery for a debt evidenced by a cheque, and is supported by the intrinsic facts of the instant transaction between the parties. The defendant issued a cheque for Rs. 10,00,000 on {{DATE}} drawn on State Bank of India, at a branch to be identified as {{BANK_BRANCH_PLACE}}, which was presented for payment and dishonoured with the endorsement “Insufficient Funds.” In pursuance of the statutory scheme, the plaintiff served a written demand notice under Section 138 NI Act dated 01 February 2025 on the defendant demanding payment of the said amount within 15 days from receipt of the notice, and the defendant has failed to comply with the demand within the statutory period. The facts establish that (a) the cheque was issued for discharge of a liability, (b) the cheque was dishonoured on presentation by the drawee bank for insufficient funds, (c) the demand notice under Section 138 NI Act was issued within the period mandated by law, and (d) the defendant did not pay within 15 days of service of the notice. Consequently, the plaintiff seeks to recover the principal sum of Rs. 10,00,000 along with interest at the rate of 18% per annum from the date the amount fell due, together with any other permissible relief. Annexure A1 contains the cheque; Annexure A2 contains the notice under Section 138 NI Act; Annexure A3 contains the bank’s dishonour endorsement.

Section 138 NI Act lays down a strict statutory framework whereby the drawer of a cheque issued in discharge of a debt or liability becomes liable for criminal punishment if the cheque is dishonoured for insufficiency of funds and the drawer fails to make payment after service of a written demand for payment within the prescribed period. The rule requires (i) a debt or liability evidenced by the cheque, (ii) dishonour of the cheque on presentation due to insufficiency of funds, (iii) service of a written demand notice upon the drawer, (iv) failure to make payment within 15 days of receipt of the notice, and (v) consequent legal proceedings, if necessary. The facts of this case satisfy each of these elements: the cheque for Rs. 10,00,000 was issued by the defendant; it was drawn on the State Bank of India and was dishonoured on presentation due to insufficient funds, as evidenced by the bank’s endorsement; the plaintiff issued a Section 138 NI Act notice dated {{DATE}}; the defendant did not pay within 15 days thereafter. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, the plaintiff avers that this constitutes a civil claim for recovery of money arising from a written instrument or contract, apart from the criminal liability under Section 138 NI Act, since civil remedies for money due on a cheque are permissible and do not fetter or bar the criminal route, and both remedies may be pursued in the appropriate forum.

As a comprehensive response to anticipated defenses, the plaintiff anticipates that the defendant might contend, first, that the criminal action under Section 138 NI Act bars civil recovery; in response, it is respectfully submitted that criminal proceedings do not operate as an exclusive remedy and that civil recovery remains available for the amount due on the dishonoured cheque. Second, the defendant may argue that there was no debt or that the cheque was not issued for consideration; the facts prove the opposite, since the cheque was issued by the defendant to discharge a liability, evidenced by the very act of issuance and delivery of the cheque for Rs. 10,00,000; third, the defendant may contend that the notice under Section 138 NI Act was defective; the notice is dated {{DATE}} and was served in accordance with the statutory requirement, and the defendant did not comply within 15 days, which constitutes the triggering event for civil action as well. Fourth, on limitation, the plaintiff contends that the cause of action accrues upon failure to comply with the notice within the 15-day period and that the suit is filed within the statutory limitation period for money suits. With respect to valuation, court fee, and jurisdiction, the plaintiff relies on the value of Rs. 10,00,000 for the principal claim, with interest at 18% per annum, and seeks appropriate court fees under the applicable Court Fees Act. The plaintiff further pleads that the District Court has jurisdiction to entertain a suit for recovery of money and to grant interest and costs.

Limitation is recognized under applicable law as to the period within which such a civil action may be filed and is calculated from the date on which the cause of action accrues, which in this case is the date on which the 15-day period following service of the Section 138 NI Act notice expires, i.e., {{DATE}}. The plaintiff reserves the right to invoke such limitation principles and to rely on any amendments or clarifications of the governing limitations regime in force at the time of trial. The plaintiff asserts a valuation of Rs. 10,00,000 for purposes of court fee and territorial/jurisdictional assessment, with further claim for interest at 18% per annum on the principal sum from the due date until realization, and the Court Fees payable shall be calculated in accordance with the applicable Court Fees Act and any subsidiary schedules.

Verification and relief are sought in the plaint, including linear relief for damages, interest, costs, and such other relief as the evidence may warrant, with the prayer that the defendant be ordered to pay the principal amount of Rs. 10,00,000 along with interest at 18% per annum, and costs of the suit. Annexures A1 to A3 are annexed herewith to prove the factual matrix set out above.

The foregoing is stated without prejudice to such further and other pleadings as may be necessary in light of any additional documents or evidence brought on record during the course of the proceedings, and the plaintiff reserves the right to amend or supplement the pleadings as may be warranted by law.

I am signing this section in my capacity as Plaintiff, and I have the authority to pursue the present suit.

filled_sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
filled_sections.text: The cause of action arises from the dishonour of the cheque dated 15.01.2025 for Rs. 10,00,000 issued by the Defendant and drawn on State Bank of India in favour of the Plaintiff, which was presented for payment and dishonoured by the drawee bank with the endorsement “Insufficient Funds” at the branch located in {{PLACE_NAMES}} on {{DISHONOUR_DATE}}, thereby creating the legal obligation to repay the amount and giving the Plaintiff the right to pursue recovery under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act. In pursuance of that right, the Plaintiff served a statutory legal notice under Section 138 NI Act dated 01.02.2025 demanding payment of Rs. 10,00,000; the Defendant failed to discharge the liability within the statutory period of fifteen days from the date of service, which sustains the Plaintiff’s claim by way of continuing default and crystallises a continuing cause of action for recovery of the principal sum and interest at 18% per annum. The cause of action is therefore live and continuing because the Defendant has not paid the amount due despite service of the statutory notice, and the liability remains enforceable in the District Court at {{PLACE_NAMES}}, with interest accumulating at 18% per annum from the date of default until realization, and nothing in the intervening period has discharged or settled the debt.

filled_sections.heading: LIMITATION
filled_sections.text: Article: {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} of the Limitation Act 1963 governs the limitation for the civil suit for recovery based on a dishonoured cheque under Section 138 NI Act. The period is three years. The cause of action accrues on {{DATE}}, being the expiry of the statutory 15-day period from the date of service of the demand notice dated {{DATE}} after the cheque dated {{DATE}} drawn on State Bank of India was dishonoured on presentation and the defendant failed to pay. The suit is within time since it is filed within three years from {{DATE}}.

filled_sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
filled_sections.text: Valuation for the purposes of court fee and jurisdiction: The Plaintiff claims Rs 10,00,000/- as the principal sum due under a cheque dated {{DATE}} drawn on the State Bank of India, which was dishonoured on presentation with endorsement 'Insufficient Funds'. The Plaintiff further states that a demand notice under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act was served on {{DATE}} and the Defendant failed to pay within 15 days, thereby giving rise to a money suit and a civil claim for recovery of the amount along with interest at 18% per annum. This is a money suit and not a property or other valuation; thus the valuation for court fee is the principal amount Rs 10,00,000; the Court fee payable under the applicable State Court Fees Act is Rs {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}. The Plaintiff seeks interest on the principal at 18% per annum from the date of default until payment or decree; this relief does not alter the valuation for court fee.

filled_sections.heading: INTEREST
filled_sections.text:
The Plaintiff states that the Defendant issued a cheque for Rs. 10,00,000 on {{DATE}}, drawn on State Bank of India, in discharge of a liability. The cheque was presented for payment and dishonoured with the endorsement “Insufficient Funds” on the date of presentment, namely {{DISHONOUR_DATE}}. Thereafter a legal notice under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act was served on the Defendant on {{DATE}}, requiring payment within 15 days, but the Defendant did not discharge the liability within the statutory period.

Pre-suit interest: The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest on Rs. 10,00,000 at 18% per annum from {{DATE}}, being the expiry of the 15-day period after service of the notice, up to the date of filing this suit, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}. The rule of law invoked is that a money-debt payable in a commercial transaction affords compensation for the use of money due and payable, and the Plaintiff is entitled to seek restitution for the interval during which the Defendant retained the Plaintiff’s funds following dishonour and notice. The rate of 18% per annum is justified as a fair market rate reflecting the risk, the Plaintiff’s reliance on the cheque, and standard commercial practice in recovery of money under negotiable instruments.

Pendente lite interest (Section 34 CPC): The Plaintiff claims pendente lite interest on the same Rs. 10,00,000 at 18% per annum from the date of filing this suit, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}, until the date of decree or final decision in the suit, i.e., {{DECREE_DATE}}. The legal basis is Section 34 CPC, which authorizes the award of interest on money decreed for the period during the pendency of the suit. The justification is to compensate the Plaintiff for the use of funds during the course of litigation and to reflect the delay caused by the Defendant’s failure to settle the liability arising from the dishonoured cheque after service of the statutory notice.

Future interest: The Plaintiff seeks post-decree interest on Rs. 10,00,000 at 18% per annum from the date of the decree until realization. The legal basis again rests on the Court’s power to award interest on a money decree to secure payment, and the rate is maintained at 18% per annum to ensure prompt recovery and discourage continued default. The end date for this period will be the date of realization or such other date as the Court may direct.

In summary, the Plaintiff prays for (i) payment of Rs. 10,00,000 principal, (ii) pre-suit interest as stated above, (iii) pendente lite interest as stated above, and (iv) future post-decree interest as stated above, all subject to such adjustments as the Court may determine in accordance with law and the evidence led at trial. The foregoing is stated in the spirit of a legitimate civil remedy in a money-debt dispute arising from a dishonoured cheque under Section 138 NI Act, for consideration by the District Court.

filled_sections.text:

a) Decree for payment of Rs. 10,00,000/- being the principal amount due under the said cheque dated 15.01.2025, drawn on State Bank of India, which was dishonoured on presentation for insufficient funds, together with interest and costs as provided herein.

b) Decree for interest at 18% per annum on Rs. 10,00,000/- from {{DATE}}, being the date on which the payee/ Plaintiff became entitled to require payment after the expiry of 15 days from the date of service of the Section 138 NI Act notice, until realization, with simple interest computed and direction for payment within a reasonable period as the Court shall deem fit.

c) Decree for costs of suit to be paid by the Defendant to the Plaintiff in the sum of Rs. {{COSTS}}/-, or such other amount as the Court may fix as costs on scale for civil suits, along with any incidental costs incurred by the Plaintiff in prosecuting this suit.

d) Any other relief that this Hon’ble Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of this case, including such further orders as may be necessary to ensure effective enforcement and realization of the amounts decreed herein.

g) General relief clause: The Plaintiff prays that the reliefs aforesaid be granted without prejudice to any other remedy available in law and that the Court may pass such other orders as may be required for the due administration of justice.

CAUSE OF ACTION

The cause of action arises from the dishonour of the cheque dated 15.01.2025 for Rs. 10,00,000 issued by the Defendant and drawn on State Bank of India in favour of the Plaintiff, which was presented for payment and dishonoured by the drawee bank with the endorsement “Insufficient Funds” at the branch located in {{PLACE_NAMES}} on {{DISHONOUR_DATE}}, thereby creating the legal obligation to repay the amount and giving the Plaintiff the right to pursue recovery under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act. In pursuance of that right, the Plaintiff served a statutory legal notice under Section 138 NI Act dated 01.02.2025 demanding payment of Rs. 10,00,000; the Defendant failed to discharge the liability within the statutory period of fifteen days from the date of service, which sustains the Plaintiff’s claim by way of continuing default and crystallises a continuing cause of action for recovery of the principal sum and interest at 18% per annum. The cause of action is therefore live and continuing because the Defendant has not paid the amount due despite service of the statutory notice, and the liability remains enforceable in the District Court at {{PLACE_NAMES}}, with interest accumulating at 18% per annum from the date of default until realization, and nothing in the intervening period has discharged or settled the debt.


LIMITATION

Article: {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} of the Limitation Act 1963 governs the limitation for the civil suit for recovery based on a dishonoured cheque under Section 138 NI Act. The period is three years. The cause of action accrues on {{DATE}}, being the expiry of the statutory 15-day period from the date of service of the demand notice dated {{DATE}} after the cheque dated {{DATE}} drawn on State Bank of India was dishonoured on presentation and the defendant failed to pay. The suit is within time since it is filed within three years from {{DATE}}.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

Valuation for the purposes of court fee and jurisdiction: The Plaintiff claims Rs 10,00,000/- as the principal sum due under a cheque dated {{DATE}} drawn on the State Bank of India, which was dishonoured on presentation with endorsement 'Insufficient Funds'. The Plaintiff further states that a demand notice under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act was served on {{DATE}} and the Defendant failed to pay within 15 days, thereby giving rise to a money suit and a civil claim for recovery of the amount along with interest at 18% per annum. This is a money suit and not a property or other valuation; thus the valuation for court fee is the principal amount Rs 10,00,000; the Court fee payable under the applicable State Court Fees Act is Rs {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}. The Plaintiff seeks interest on the principal at 18% per annum from the date of default until payment or decree; this relief does not alter the valuation for court fee.


INTEREST

The Plaintiff states that the Defendant issued a cheque for Rs. 10,00,000 on {{DATE}}, drawn on State Bank of India, in discharge of a liability. The cheque was presented for payment and dishonoured with the endorsement “Insufficient Funds” on the date of presentment, namely {{DISHONOUR_DATE}}. Thereafter a legal notice under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act was served on the Defendant on {{DATE}}, requiring payment within 15 days, but the Defendant did not discharge the liability within the statutory period.

Pre-suit interest: The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest on Rs. 10,00,000 at 18% per annum from {{DATE}}, being the expiry of the 15-day period after service of the notice, up to the date of filing this suit, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}. The rule of law invoked is that a money-debt payable in a commercial transaction affords compensation for the use of money due and payable, and the Plaintiff is entitled to seek restitution for the interval during which the Defendant retained the Plaintiff’s funds following dishonour and notice. The rate of 18% per annum is justified as a fair market rate reflecting the risk, the Plaintiff’s reliance on the cheque, and standard commercial practice in recovery of money under negotiable instruments.

Pendente lite interest (Section 34 CPC): The Plaintiff claims pendente lite interest on the same Rs. 10,00,000 at 18% per annum from the date of filing this suit, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}, until the date of decree or final decision in the suit, i.e., {{DECREE_DATE}}. The legal basis is Section 34 CPC, which authorizes the award of interest on money decreed for the period during the pendency of the suit. The justification is to compensate the Plaintiff for the use of funds during the course of litigation and to reflect the delay caused by the Defendant’s failure to settle the liability arising from the dishonoured cheque after service of the statutory notice.

Future interest: The Plaintiff seeks post-decree interest on Rs. 10,00,000 at 18% per annum from the date of the decree until realization. The legal basis again rests on the Court’s power to award interest on a money decree to secure payment, and the rate is maintained at 18% per annum to ensure prompt recovery and discourage continued default. The end date for this period will be the date of realization or such other date as the Court may direct.

In summary, the Plaintiff prays for (i) payment of Rs. 10,00,000 principal, (ii) pre-suit interest as stated above, (iii) pendente lite interest as stated above, and (iv) future post-decree interest as stated above, all subject to such adjustments as the Court may determine in accordance with law and the evidence led at trial. The foregoing is stated in the spirit of a legitimate civil remedy in a money-debt dispute arising from a dishonoured cheque under Section 138 NI Act, for consideration by the District Court.


PRAYER

The Plaintiff prays for the following reliefs in respect of the suit for recovery on a dishonoured cheque, it being understood that the Defendant issued a cheque for Rs. 10,00,000/- dated 15.01.2025 drawn on State Bank of India, which was dishonoured on presentation with the endorsement 'Insufficient Funds', and a legal notice under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act was served on 01.02.2025 but the Defendant did not avail the statutory period to make payment.

a) Decree for payment of Rs. 10,00,000/- being the principal amount due under the said cheque dated 15.01.2025, drawn on State Bank of India, which was dishonoured on presentation for insufficient funds, together with interest and costs as provided herein.

b) Decree for interest at 18% per annum on Rs. 10,00,000/- from {{DATE}}, being the date on which the payee/ Plaintiff became entitled to require payment after the expiry of 15 days from the date of service of the Section 138 NI Act notice, until realization, with simple interest computed and direction for payment within a reasonable period as the Court shall deem fit.

c) Decree for costs of suit to be paid by the Defendant to the Plaintiff in the sum of Rs. {{COSTS}}/-, or such other amount as the Court may fix as costs on scale for civil suits, along with any incidental costs incurred by the Plaintiff in prosecuting this suit.

d) Any other relief that this Hon’ble Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of this case, including such further orders as may be necessary to ensure effective enforcement and realization of the amounts decreed herein.

g) General relief clause: The Plaintiff prays that the reliefs aforesaid be granted without prejudice to any other remedy available in law and that the Court may pass such other orders as may be required for the due administration of justice.

LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

6. Annexure A — Original Cheque: The Defendant issued to the Plaintiff a cheque for Rs. 10,00,000 dated {{DATE}}, drawn on State Bank of India, which was delivered to the Plaintiff and presented for encashment; the instrument is annexed as Annexure A.

7. Annexure B — Dishonour Memo: The cheque was presented for payment to the drawee bank and dishonoured with the endorsement 'Insufficient Funds' by the drawee bank; the bank's return memo is annexed as Annexure B, with the dishonour date shown as {{DISHONOUR_DATE}}.

8. Annexure C — Legal Notice under Section 138 NI Act: On 01 February 2025 the Plaintiff served a legal notice under Section 138 NI Act upon the Defendant at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}} demanding payment within 15 days; the notice is annexed as Annexure C.

9. Annexure D — Proof of Service of Notice: Service of the legal notice is evidenced by postal receipts and acknowledgment; the service details are annexed as Annexure D, with the service date shown as {{SERVICE_DATE}}.

10. Annexure E — Ledger/Accounts: The Plaintiff’s books of account and ledger extracts show the liability of Rs. 10,00,000 to the Plaintiff from the Defendant, corroborating the claim arising from the dishonoured cheque; the ledger extracts are annexed as Annexure E.

IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}}
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} / {{SUIT_YEAR}}

IN THE DISTRICT COURT AT {{PLACE}}

Plaintiff: {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}

Defendant: {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. 10,00,000/-

Cause of action arises from the following events: On 15.01.2025 the Defendant issued a cheque for Rs. 10,00,000/- drawn on State Bank of India; the cheque was presented and dishonoured with the endorsement 'Insufficient Funds'. The Plaintiff served a legal notice under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act on 01.02.2025 demanding payment of the amount; the Defendant failed to pay within the statutory period of 15 days. The Plaintiff thus files this suit seeking recovery of the principal sum together with interest at 18% per annum from {{DATE}} until realization, and costs. Limitation: The suit is instituted within the period of limitation as provided by law, the cause of action arising on {{DATE}} and the suit being filed within three years thereof. Valuation and court fee: The suit is valued for the purpose of court fee at Rs. 10,00,000/-, Court Fee: Rs. {{COURT_FEE}}. Verification: I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff, do hereby verify that the contents of this plaint are true and correct to the best of my knowledge and belief. Verified on {{VERIFICATION_DATE}} at {{PLACE}}.


BETWEEN

Plaintiff: {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}} (hereinafter referred to as the 'Plaintiff'), is sui juris and competent to sue. Defendant: {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}} (hereinafter referred to as the 'Defendant'), is competent to be sued. The Plaintiff files this suit for recovery of Rs. 10,00,000/- arising from a cheque dated 15.01.2025 drawn on State Bank of India by the Defendant, which on presentation was dishonoured with the endorsement 'Insufficient Funds'. On 01.02.2025 the Plaintiff served a legal notice under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act; however, the Defendant failed to make payment within the statutory period of 15 days.


JURISDICTION

The territorial jurisdiction for filing this suit lies with the District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}} because the transaction giving rise to the cause of action occurred within this district and the statutory preconditions for initiating a Section 138 NI Act action were satisfied within this district, including the service of the legal notice here and the dishonour of the cheque here. On 15.01.2025 the Defendant issued a cheque for Rs. 10,00,000 drawn on State Bank of India in favour of the Plaintiff. The cheque was dishonoured on presentation by the drawee bank with the endorsement “Insufficient Funds” at a bank located in this district. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 01.02.2025 under Section 138 NI Act to the Defendant at an address within this district, and the Defendant did not pay the amount within 15 days from the date of service. Accordingly, the cause of action arose within the territorial limits of this court, and the District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}} has territorial jurisdiction to adjudicate the civil recovery claim arising from the same transaction.

Pecuniary jurisdiction is satisfied since the suit seeks recovery of Rs. 10,00,000 as principal amount, with interest at 18% per annum. The total value of the claim, including interest, is within the monetary ceiling of the District Court in {{STATE}} for suits of this nature; the suit is valued at Rs. 10,00,000 for the principal amount, and the court’s pecuniary limits permit adjudication of claims within this range. Therefore, this court has the requisite pecuniary jurisdiction to entertain and try the suit.

Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}


FACTS OF THE CASE

1. On {{DATE}}, the Defendant issued to the Plaintiff a cheque for Rs. 10,00,000 drawn on the State Bank of India and handed over the instrument at {{PLACE_NAME}} in discharge of the Defendant's obligation under their financial arrangement. (Annexure A)

2. On a date prior to {{DATE}}, the Plaintiff presented the cheque for payment to the drawee bank, State Bank of India, at {{CHEQUE_PRESENTATION_PLACE}}, whereupon the bank returned the instrument unpaid with the endorsement "Insufficient Funds" on the dishonour memo dated {{DISHONOR_DATE}}. (Annexure B)

3. On {{DATE}}, the Plaintiff served the Defendant with a legal notice under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act at the Defendant's address {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}} demanding payment of Rs. 10,00,000 within 15 days. The notice is evidenced by Annexure C and service by delivery/acknowledgement is evidenced by Annexure D.

4. Despite service, the Defendant did not pay the amount within the statutory period, i.e., no payment was received by {{DATE}}, and the outstanding sum remains unpaid. (Annexure D)

5. On {{FILING_DATE}}, the Plaintiff filed this suit before the District Court at {{PLACE_NAME}} seeking recovery of Rs. 10,00,000 and interest at 18% per annum from the date of default, relying on the facts set out above. The Plaintiff has valued the suit at Rs. 10,00,000 for purposes of court fee and jurisdiction, has paid the prescribed court fee, and has appended a verification clause to the plaint. Annexures E (Verification) and F (Court Fee and Valuation) are attached to support these procedural aspects. (Annexure E, Annexure F)


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

This suit is founded on the statutory remedy available under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, read with the ordinary civil law governing money recovery for a debt evidenced by a cheque, and is supported by the intrinsic facts of the instant transaction between the parties. The defendant issued a cheque for Rs. 10,00,000 on {{DATE}} drawn on State Bank of India, at a branch to be identified as {{BANK_BRANCH_PLACE}}, which was presented for payment and dishonoured with the endorsement “Insufficient Funds.” In pursuance of the statutory scheme, the plaintiff served a written demand notice under Section 138 NI Act dated 01 February 2025 on the defendant demanding payment of the said amount within 15 days from receipt of the notice, and the defendant has failed to comply with the demand within the statutory period. The facts establish that (a) the cheque was issued for discharge of a liability, (b) the cheque was dishonoured on presentation by the drawee bank for insufficient funds, (c) the demand notice under Section 138 NI Act was issued within the period mandated by law, and (d) the defendant did not pay within 15 days of service of the notice. Consequently, the plaintiff seeks to recover the principal sum of Rs. 10,00,000 along with interest at the rate of 18% per annum from the date the amount fell due, together with any other permissible relief. Annexure A1 contains the cheque; Annexure A2 contains the notice under Section 138 NI Act; Annexure A3 contains the bank’s dishonour endorsement.

Section 138 NI Act lays down a strict statutory framework whereby the drawer of a cheque issued in discharge of a debt or liability becomes liable for criminal punishment if the cheque is dishonoured for insufficiency of funds and the drawer fails to make payment after service of a written demand for payment within the prescribed period. The rule requires (i) a debt or liability evidenced by the cheque, (ii) dishonour of the cheque on presentation due to insufficiency of funds, (iii) service of a written demand notice upon the drawer, (iv) failure to make payment within 15 days of receipt of the notice, and (v) consequent legal proceedings, if necessary. The facts of this case satisfy each of these elements: the cheque for Rs. 10,00,000 was issued by the defendant; it was drawn on the State Bank of India and was dishonoured on presentation due to insufficient funds, as evidenced by the bank’s endorsement; the plaintiff issued a Section 138 NI Act notice dated {{DATE}}; the defendant did not pay within 15 days thereafter. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, the plaintiff avers that this constitutes a civil claim for recovery of money arising from a written instrument or contract, apart from the criminal liability under Section 138 NI Act, since civil remedies for money due on a cheque are permissible and do not fetter or bar the criminal route, and both remedies may be pursued in the appropriate forum.

As a comprehensive response to anticipated defenses, the plaintiff anticipates that the defendant might contend, first, that the criminal action under Section 138 NI Act bars civil recovery; in response, it is respectfully submitted that criminal proceedings do not operate as an exclusive remedy and that civil recovery remains available for the amount due on the dishonoured cheque. Second, the defendant may argue that there was no debt or that the cheque was not issued for consideration; the facts prove the opposite, since the cheque was issued by the defendant to discharge a liability, evidenced by the very act of issuance and delivery of the cheque for Rs. 10,00,000; third, the defendant may contend that the notice under Section 138 NI Act was defective; the notice is dated {{DATE}} and was served in accordance with the statutory requirement, and the defendant did not comply within 15 days, which constitutes the triggering event for civil action as well. Fourth, on limitation, the plaintiff contends that the cause of action accrues upon failure to comply with the notice within the 15-day period and that the suit is filed within the statutory limitation period for money suits. With respect to valuation, court fee, and jurisdiction, the plaintiff relies on the value of Rs. 10,00,000 for the principal claim, with interest at 18% per annum, and seeks appropriate court fees under the applicable Court Fees Act. The plaintiff further pleads that the District Court has jurisdiction to entertain a suit for recovery of money and to grant interest and costs.

Limitation is recognized under applicable law as to the period within which such a civil action may be filed and is calculated from the date on which the cause of action accrues, which in this case is the date on which the 15-day period following service of the Section 138 NI Act notice expires, i.e., {{DATE}}. The plaintiff reserves the right to invoke such limitation principles and to rely on any amendments or clarifications of the governing limitations regime in force at the time of trial. The plaintiff asserts a valuation of Rs. 10,00,000 for purposes of court fee and territorial/jurisdictional assessment, with further claim for interest at 18% per annum on the principal sum from the due date until realization, and the Court Fees payable shall be calculated in accordance with the applicable Court Fees Act and any subsidiary schedules.

Verification and relief are sought in the plaint, including linear relief for damages, interest, costs, and such other relief as the evidence may warrant, with the prayer that the defendant be ordered to pay the principal amount of Rs. 10,00,000 along with interest at 18% per annum, and costs of the suit. Annexures A1 to A3 are annexed herewith to prove the factual matrix set out above.

The foregoing is stated without prejudice to such further and other pleadings as may be necessary in light of any additional documents or evidence brought on record during the course of the proceedings, and the plaintiff reserves the right to amend or supplement the pleadings as may be warranted by law.

Verification Clause: I, the Plaintiff, do hereby verify that the contents of this Legal Basis and Grounds section are true to the best of my knowledge and belief, and that no material facts have been suppressed. I am signing this section in my capacity as Plaintiff, and I have the authority to pursue the present suit.

Annexure references: Annexure A1 — Copy of the cheque dated 15.01.2025; Annexure A2 — Notice under Section 138 NI Act dated 01.02.2025; Annexure A3 — Bank endorsement showing insufficient funds; Annexure A4 — Proof of service of notice (if available); Annexure A5 — Any other documentary support.


CAUSE OF ACTION

The cause of action arises from the dishonour of the cheque dated 15.01.2025 for Rs. 10,00,000 issued by the Defendant and drawn on State Bank of India in favour of the Plaintiff, which was presented for payment and dishonoured by the drawee bank with the endorsement “Insufficient Funds” at the branch located in {{PLACE_NAMES}} on {{DISHONOUR_DATE}}, thereby creating the legal obligation to repay the amount and giving the Plaintiff the right to pursue recovery under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act. The Plaintiff seeks interest on the principal at 18% per annum from the date of default until payment or decree; this relief does not alter the valuation for court fee.

Pre-suit interest: The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest on Rs. 10,00,000 at 18% per annum from {{DATE}}, being the expiry of the 15-day period after service of the notice, up to the date of filing this suit, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}. The rate of 18% per annum is justified as a fair market rate reflecting the risk, the Plaintiff’s reliance on the cheque, and standard commercial practice in recovery of money under negotiable instruments.

Pendente lite interest (Section 34 CPC): The Plaintiff claims pendente lite interest on the same Rs. 10,00,000 at 18% per annum from the date of filing this suit, i.e., {{FILING_DATE}}, until the date of decree or final decision in the suit, i.e., {{DECREE_DATE}}. In summary, the Plaintiff prays for (i) payment of Rs. 10,00,000 principal, (ii) pre-suit interest as stated above, (iii) pendente lite interest as stated above, and (iv) future post-decree interest as stated above, all subject to such adjustments as the Court may determine in accordance with law and the evidence led at trial. The foregoing is stated in the spirit of a legitimate civil remedy in a money-debt dispute arising from a dishonoured cheque under Section 138 NI Act, for consideration by the District Court.


PRAYER

LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
